data.title: D.R. Horton: Take Part In The Real Estate Boom, Upon A Correction
data.description:
DHI Remains Attractively Valued For The Upcoming Real Estate Boom, After A Moderate Correction

We previously covered D.R. Horton, Inc. (NYSE:DHI) in March 2024, discussing why we had maintained our Buy rating, attributed to the robust sales and performance metrics compared to pre-pandemic levels.

This was on top of the healthier balance sheet and secure dividend investment thesis, with it allowing opportunistic investors to take advantage of the recent pullback and the reasonably priced stock.

DHI YTD Stock Price

Since then, DHI has already rallied by +19.5%, compared to the wider market at +13.2% - with the same exuberance also observed in its US homebuilding peers.

Much of the recovery is attributed to the stock market rotation from high growth to value/ dividend stocks in July 2024, significantly aided by the Fed's recent pivot by 50 basis points in the September 2024 FOMC meeting.

The latter has already contributed to the moderating 30Y Fixed Rate Mortgage Average to 6.32% and the 15Y rate to 5.41% by October 10, 2024, down drastically from the peak of 7.79% and 7.03% observed on October 26, 2023, respectively.

As a result of the lower borrowing costs, it is unsurprising that Redfin (RDFN), the online real estate brokerage company, has already reported a sudden increase in mortgage-rate locks by +68% MoM by September 23, 2024.

This is on top of RDFN's Homebuyer Demand Index (a measure of tours and other buying services from Redfin agents) rising drastically to nearly "its highest level since May (2024)" as of October 10, 2024 - with mortgage-purchase applications also going up by +8% MoM, building upon the +10% MoM expansion observed by September 23, 2024.

These developments matter indeed, since the lower borrowing costs may trigger higher home buying activities, with it already contributing to the DHI stock's robust performance on a YTD basis.

At the same time, the median sales price gap between existing homes and new houses has narrowed drastically to only ~1% at between $416.7K and $420.6K by August 2024, respectively, compared to 30.8% observed in December 2022 and 20% in December 2019.

Readers must also note that DHI has reported an average selling price of approximately $380K per home in the latest earnings call, with it underscoring its near-term tailwinds as the largest homebuilding company in the US.

As a result of these promising market trends and the nearing of "magic mortgage rates of 5%," we can understand why the management has raised their FY2024 revenue guidance to $37B at the midpoint (+4.2% YoY) and home closings to 90.25K (+7.34K YoY).

This is up from the original guidance of $36.5B (+2.8% YoY) and 87.5K (+4.59K YoY) offered in the FQ4'23 earnings call, respectively.

If recent market developments are of any indication, we may see DHI report another double beat performance in the upcoming FQ4'24 earnings call scheduled on October 29, 2024, building upon the historical consecutive beats over the past 7 quarters (barring a one-time minimal adj EPS miss in FQ1'24).

This is especially since the homebuilder has 8.8K unsold completed homes and 17.4K in progress homes at highly favorable price points as of the latest quarter, allowing them to partake in the upcoming real estate boom.

The same has been hinted by the DHI management, based on the FQ4'24 home sales gross margin guidance of 24% (inline QoQ/ -1 YoY) compared to FQ3'23 levels of 24% (+0.9 points QoQ/ +0.7 YoY), YTD levels of 23.3% (+0.4 points YoY), and FY2019 levels of 20.3% (-1.1 points YoY).

This is on top of the 24.25K homes sales expected in FQ4'24 (+5.4% QoQ/ +5.8% YoY) - with the home builder likely to offer a similarly robust FY2025 sales guidance as the Fed continues on their rate normalization path.

Readers cannot forget DHI's well diversified operations across rental homes, mortgage financing, title services, and insurance agency services for its homebuyers as well.

The rate cut may allow the homebuilder to report higher (and less risky) loan originations, significantly aided by the stronger FICO scores at 725 and the higher buyer incomes at approximately $100K as of July 2024.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result, we can understand why the consensus has expected DHI to record a robust FY2025 and FY2026 sales performance at high single digits, albeit still decelerating from its 5Y pre-pandemic top/ bottom-line expansion at a CAGR of +17%/ +21.9% and its 10Y normalized averages of +18.9%/ +26.4% (partly aided by acquisitions), respectively.

Despite the growth moderation, we believe that dividend oriented investors need not fret, since the homebuilder remains more than well capitalized to return great value to existing shareholders.

This is attributed to the management's FY2024 cash flow guidance of $3B (-30.2% YoY/ +237% from FY2019 levels of $892M), compared to the LTM dividend obligations of $381.8M (+13.9% sequentially) and LTM share repurchases of $1.73B (+60.1% sequentially), while building upon the CEO's highly promising guidance:

But we do expect that as cash flow does provide a significant increase next year (FY2025), we will increase our repurchases and dividends proportionately to that. (Seeking Alpha)

DHI Valuations

And this is also why we believe that DHI does not appear to be overly expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 12.57x, compared to its 5Y mean of 9.13x and 10Y mean of 10.53x.

This is attributed to its reasonably valued PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.91x compared to the sector median of 1.54x and its direct peers, including Lennar Corporation (LEN) at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.80x and PulteGroup, Inc. (PHM) at 0.56x, with it offering interested investors with a decent margin of safety.

So, Is DHI Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

DHI 5Y Stock Price

For now, DHI has already run away from its 100/ 200 day moving averages, while charting new peaks in September 2024.

Based on the LTM adj EPS of $14.89 ending FQ3'24 and the 10Y P/E non-GAAP mean of 10.53x, it appears that the stock has run away from our updated fair value estimates of $156.80.

Based on the consensus FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $17.69, there remains a minimal margin of safety to our long-term price target of $186.20 as well - with it apparent that the market has gotten over exuberant now.

The recent rally has also triggered DHI's lower forward yields of 0.66%, compared to its 5Y mean of 1.02% and the sector median of 2.25%, with it triggering a relatively underwhelming dividend investment thesis.

If anything, with the stock currently retesting the Q3'24 support levels of $180s after failing to break out of the all-time heights of $197s, we believe that it is likely to further pull back to its August 2024 support levels of $160s - with those levels also nearer to our fair value estimates.

As a result, while we are maintaining our Buy rating for the DHI stock, it is with the caveat that interested investors wait for the retracement to occur and only add at $160s for an improved margin of safety.

We believe that a market wide correction is bound to happen as the stock market enters the uncertain Q3'24 earnings season while boasting an increasingly greedy market sentiments and elevated CBOE Volatility Index.

Patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: 2 Reasons Why Taking Profits Is Prudent Today (Downgrade)
data.description:
There was a great financial media hoopla about the S&P 500's 2nd bull market anniversary. Tech stocks attracted much of the attention given their massive run-up following the launch of ChatGPT in November 2022. But another niche has surprised many investors with relative strength, including a recent burst of upside.

The Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX) has generally kept up with the SPX's torrid pace in the last 24 months. The pipeline stock fund is now near all-time highs on a total return basis amid volatility in the oil market. One of the ETF's biggest weights, Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) is close to 52-week highs on its own and is up 47% in total return in 2024.

I am downgrading the company from a buy to a hold. Shares have returned 48% since my February buy rating. I now see the stock near fair value with a stretched technical chart. It reports Q3 results tonight.

Midstream Infrastructure ETF Soars 66% (Total Return) Last 2 Years

Back in July, KMI, the largest operator of carbon dioxide pipelines in North America, reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q2 GAAP EPS of $0.26 beat the Wall Street consensus forecast by a penny. Revenue of $3.6 billion, up 2% from the same period a year earlier, was a significant $520 million miss. The management team approved a $0.2875 cash dividend and KMI had a distributable cash flow of $1.1 billion, just a modest sequential increase.

As of July, the firm sees budgeted net income attributable to KMI of $2.7 billion, or $1.22 per share, which would be a 15% increase from FY 2023. Total dividends are seen at $1.15 this year, a 2% potential rise.

Shares rose 2.5% in the session that followed. After the Q2 report, there have been more negative sell-side earnings revisions than positive, and energy prices have been volatile. Of course, this $55 billion market cap midstream stock is much less sensitive to ebbs and flows in oil and gas prices.

BofA recently upgraded shares from a hold to a buy, putting a new $27 price target on the stock. They cited potential long-term growth from Gulf Coast LNG projects and increased demand from AI and utility electrification and generally benefitting from new gas pipeline projects. The company has geographical diversification, which could help mitigate any post-election risks.

Looking ahead to tonight's earnings report, the options market has priced in a small 2.5% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle, expiring soonest after the release. That's about in line with the 2.3% average, per data from Option Research & Technology Services (ORATS). Seeking Alpha shows a consensus non-GAAP EPS figure of $0.27 on revenue of $4.05 billion.

Key risks for the company include weaker demand for LNG natural gas projects, unfavorable regulatory outcomes, particularly after the US elections, and higher interest rates that could result in a greater interest expense as KMI has $32 billion in total debt.

On the earnings outlook, analysts forecast a solid 12% rise in operating EPS this year, with more modest percentage gains in the out year and through 2026. Revenue, meanwhile, is seen increasing at a rate between 3% and 6%. There may be an upside to those broad outlooks if BofA's sanguine outlook comes to fruition - we may know more tonight. Free cash flow is not overly impressive at $0.94 over the past 12 months, though.

KMI: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revisions Trends

I see KMI as a solid company with a favorable outlook. On valuation, however, we run into a few stumbling blocks. The stock now features a forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 20.6, more than four turns more expensive than its 5-year average.

I noted back in Q1 that the multiple was to the cheap side, but that can no longer be said. If we assume $1.26 of EPS in the next year and apply an 18 multiple, above its 5-year average, then the intrinsic value is just below $23. Shares also trade expensively on a price-to-sales basis.

With high share-price momentum, the stock could keep running, but I will note later that the technical situation is also stretched.

KMI: Stretched Valuation Metrics

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows a confirmed Q3 2024 earnings date of Wednesday, October 16 AMC with a conference call immediately after the results hit the tape.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With KMI shares modestly expensive after a stellar rally in 2024, the chart also shows some risks given the bullish fervor. Notice in the chart below that the stock is now about 40% above its long-term 200-day moving average. That's very extended for what's often a not-so-volatile stock. Moreover, if we take the previous trading range from the last few years - between $15.80 and $20.20, and add that on top of the breakout point of the low $20s, then the measured move price objective is very close to the recent high above $25.

KMI's RSI momentum oscillator is firmly in overbought conditions, while the 50-day moving average is way down under $22. That $22 spot is interesting in my eye, considering it's where KMI put in a short-term peak in July. I would be a technical buyer of the stock if it were to revisit $22. For now, though, the move appears extended, with a pullback possible into year-end.

KMI: Shares Reach Their Upside Price Objective, Eyeing $22 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on KMI for two reasons. There's an emerging growth story with rising LNG demand and large infrastructure spending across the country. But with (1) a 20 P/E and (2) a near-parabolic chart, taking profits here is prudent.

data.title: FTSE 100: When Rates Drop, Stocks Pop
data.description:
Stocks Don't Always Pop

In theory, stock prices should increase as interest rates drop. This is due to three main reasons. The first being that lower rates usually disincentives consumers from saving cash and results in more spending instead, thereby leading to higher revenue for companies. The second is that the cost of borrowing also decreases. These two reasons alone would, therefore, have a positive impact on companies’ earnings, and subsequently, their share prices.

But perhaps most importantly, safer assets such as cash and government bonds begin to yield lower returns as rates drop. This then incentives investors to rotate into equities, as the opportunity cost of holding on to the former would be greater than the latter in a lower-rate environment. As such, it would only make sense to see a consistent trend of equities increasing in value when central banks cut rates, and the opposite when central banks raise rates.

Be that as it may, this isn’t always the case. According to research from Morningstar, US stocks have only had positive returns in two of the last four rate-cutting cycles in the 12 months after the first rate cut. This is because a rate-cutting cycle may come as a result of a recession, whose impact will have a greater effect on earnings than rate cuts will. This is why the Dot Com and housing market bubbles in 2001 and 2007 resulted in negative returns for US equities.

Nonetheless, it’s worth noting that the UK's headline index, the FTSE 100 (UKX), has seen an exception to this rule, as it rose by healthy double digits in 1990-1991 despite a recession. Still, the prevailing theory remains true – British stocks, on average, have done well when rate cuts are in motion, especially in the 12 months after the first rate cut.

Hence, with the slim likelihood of the UK entering a recession, the odds of UK equities going up in value over the next year are pretty decent – not just based on historical trends, but also based on their cheap valuations, and the potential for global investors to rotate into European equities for greater value.

British Bonanza

The sample size for the FTSE 100’s performance in past rate-cutting cycles isn’t enormous by any means. Nevertheless, it’s still decent enough to ascertain that UK stocks tend to do well after the first rate cut is instigated.

Our data shows that average returns tend to increase as the months go by. From an average of 2.2% within the first 3 months of a first rate cut, to 7.3% within 6 months, and 9.3% within 12 months – this matches the average return of the S&P 500 over the last 30 years. That said, it’s worth noting that these figures would be higher had it not been for the two instances where the Dot Com bubble burst and the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) occurred, in 2001 and 2008, respectively.

Even so, the trend that stocks go down during a recession isn’t always true with UK stocks, as mentioned earlier. That’s because in 1990, UK large-caps still experienced a boon, gaining an average of 22.9% in the 12 months after the first rate, despite having a sluggish start in the first 3 months.

However, when the UK avoids a recession/bubble-popping event during a rate-cutting cycle, returns are generally rather plum. These instances occurred in 1996-1997 and 1998-1999, where the average return was 31.5%. This came as lower borrowing costs paired with a higher risk appetite due to a lack of economic turmoil, generated favourable market conditions for risk assets to thrive.

Bank on Tech and Consumer Cyclicals?

So, which sectors perform best then? Well, including recessions and the Dot Com bubble, it’s no shock to see the technology sector outperforming by far, with a 1Y median average return of 37.1%.

This is down to one main reason – tech stocks tend to be growth stocks which are by nature, more risky than value plays. Therefore, in a lower interest rate environment, investors and traders alike are more willing to take on risk, especially when other assets like bonds are yielding lower returns. Hence why the likes of Relx (RELX), Sage (OTCPK:SGGEF), and WPP (WPP) outperformed during these periods.

The tech sector’s outperformance is followed closely by banks, which averaged a median 1Y return of 33.0%. This comes as no surprise, given that banks tend to go into distress when rates get jacked up. This is because although their income expands from higher interest, they are also likely to see those gains getting offset by much higher impairment charges. This was even more pronounced before liquidity and loan regulations instilled after the GFC.

But as rates decline, banks benefit. This is due to a strategy called structural hedging – when banks lock in higher yields by investing in long-term bonds which continue to generate high interest, despite paying less interest to customers as rates come down.

In addition to that, as impairment charges subside too, margins could be maintained as bad debts fell. Although, it's worth noting that this wasn’t the case during the GFC when bad debts skyrocketed, causing big banks like Lloyds (LYG), Barclays (BCS), NatWest (NWG), and many more to go into free fall.

Next on the list is consumer cyclicals, with a 1Y median return of 20.2%. This is also a sensible result, as cheaper borrowing cost usually sparks consumer sentiment, and consequently, consumer spending.

As most consumer cyclical stocks get less love during times of high interest as consumers rotate their spending into purchasing staples instead, a renewed feel-good factor that comes with rate cuts usually sparks a resurgence in sales for more discretionary items. As a result, the likes of AB Foods (OTCPK:ASBFY) and Kingfisher (OTCQX:KGFHF), amongst many, tend to see customers flock back to their stores.

Cyclical Performance

These underlying trends are even more pronounced when taking the exclusive sample in the data that excludes recessions (1996-1997 & 1998-1999) into account, as tech generated an average 1Y return of 76.1%, with banks not too far behind with a 60.2% return.

Having said that, consumer cyclical stocks don't see the same love when a recession doesn’t occur, only eking out an average 1Y return of 14.3%. This is probably due to the fact that the year-on-year growth in sales isn’t as pronounced, as consumers never had to ditch spending on discretionary items to begin with. Instead, telecommunication stocks took its place, outperforming tremendously in the mid-to-late 90s, with an average 1Y return of 57.5%.

History to Repeat Itself?

All this leads to the question of whether history can repeat itself. Can UK stocks outperform over the next year given that the Bank of England just triggered its first rate cut in August? Whilst there are parallels that can be drawn, there are also reasons as to why UK equities may not outperform as they have in the past despite not entering recession.

This is primarily due to a number of headwinds. These include the uncertainty surrounding the new Labour government’s tax and growth plans, a stock market that’s now heavily reliant on China’s growth, and consistent equity outflows from the UK stock market.

Moreover, it’s crucial to highlight that the global economy is completely different now than it was back then. Telecoms had a great run up in the late 90s as the internet had just broken ground then. But with artificial intelligence now the new kid on the block and the likes of BT (OTCPK:BTGOF) and Vodafone (VOD) laden with high levels of debt and lacklustre demand as compared to their prime days, earnings growth is likely to remain muted.

Meanwhile, mining and industrial stocks are now heavily reliant on China, which is currently going through its own set of economic challenges. Although the Chinese government’s recent monetary stimulus measures have propped up some of the miners' share prices from their lows, the medium-term sustainability of the appreciation in their appreciation remains ambiguous and on a knife’s edge, as a further rally will require more economic activity, and not just stimulus.

That being said, there are tailwinds that remain considerably stronger due to the cheap valuations that UK stocks have an offer as compared to their American counterparts. This is also why our base case remains that we expect UK stocks, especially large caps, to grow by mid-to-high single percentages over the next 12 months.

We anticipate there to be a continued recovery in bank stocks as well as housebuilders like Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODF) and Persimmon (OTCPK:PSMMF), which we see as the primary winners of the current rate-cutting cycle. We also see upside potential for consumer cyclicals, particularly in apparel and premium grocers like Marks and Spencer (OTCQX:MAKSF) and Ocado (OTCPK:OCDGF).

Alongside this, we see potential for the luxury sector to rebound, albeit heavily dependent on China. If this does happen, we think it will be back-loaded to the latter half of the 12-month window/in Q2 of 2025. In the event that it does, we expect this to be driven by a rebound in the Chinese economy, which would see miners and industrials rally in tandem as well.

To conclude, even though the response of UK stocks to rate cuts may not match the dramatic gains seen in some historical cycles, our overall outlook for the FTSE 100 remains cautiously positive. Because of this, investors should consider the unique current economic landscape and sector-specific opportunities available to them while they last. As Warren Buffett once said, “Price is what you pay; value is what you get", and that couldn't be more true today.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Jazz Pharmaceuticals: Unexpected Wins Improve The Long-Term Outlook
data.description:
Investors have shown little appreciation for two recent wins of Jazz Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:JAZZ), but I believe both improve the long-term outlook for the company. The first was a partial legal win against competitor Avadel Pharmaceuticals (AVDL) in late August, where Jazz was not able to get Avadel’s Lumryz off the narcolepsy market, but it was able to lock up the idiopathic hypersomnia market (though I should add that decisions are not final and that Avadel is appealing). The second win was this week's surprising positive outcome from the phase 3 trial of Zepzelca in combination with Tecentriq as maintenance therapy in patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer.

After the minor but expected pipeline setback that I covered in my previous article, it is good to see that there is some upside to my investment thesis as my assumption was that Zepzelca was more likely than not to fail in the mentioned trial, and my baseline assumption was that Jazz would not be able to stop Avadel from selling Lumryz in either indication.

Phase 3 win could open up a large market for Zepzelca

I was surprised to see the press release where Jazz said Zepzelca (lurbinectedin) in combination with Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) Tecentriq (atezolizumab) achieved a statistically significant benefit on both endpoints of overall survival ('OS') and progression-free survival ('PFS') compared to Tecentriq monotherapy in patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer. My baseline assumption was that this trial would fail – there was no available clinical data supporting a successful outcome, and Zepzelca also failed in a confirmatory trial in the current second-line small cell lung cancer indication.

We have to wait to see the actual data before making estimates about the impact of the positive results with greater precision, but I believe it is safe to say that Zepzelca’s annual sales potential has more than doubled.

First, there are approximately 19,000 first-line ES-SCLC patients in the United States compared to 17,000 second-line patients that are eligible to receive Zepzelca monotherapy today.

Second, first-line patients are likely to stay on Zepzelca and Tecentriq longer than on Zepzelca monotherapy in the second-line setting, resulting in higher net sales per patient. For example, Zepzelca's median progression-free survival in second line SCLC is only 3.5 months, meaning patients stay on Zepzelca for a very short period of time since the drug is taken until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.

It is reasonably likely that the progression-free survival in first-line maintenance setting in ES-SCLC patients is longer than 3.5 months. For example, Tecentriq achieved a PFS of 5.5 months in this population, and the phase 3 results of the combination with Zepzelca have reportedly extended both PFS and OS of Tecentriq. As such, I believe that PFS is at least in the 6-7-month range.

And third, Zepzelca showing an extension of PFS and OS as opposed to failing to show a treatment benefit in the confirmatory trial in second-line SCLC patients should lead to higher market penetration rates in this patient population.

Zepzelca generated $81 million in net sales in Q2 2024, a 15% increase over the same quarter last year, and I will wait for the presentation of efficacy and safety data from this trial to make a more precise estimate, but as a placeholder, I believe the potential approval of the Zepzelca and Tecentriq combination could add at least $400-500 million in Zepzelca annual sales by the end of the decade, and potentially significantly more if the progression-free survival and overall survival benefits are significantly better than what Zepzelca monotherapy was able to achieve in second-line small cell lung cancer.

Jazz expects to submit the sNDA for the combination of Zepzelca and Tecentriq in the first half of 2025, and the submission could receive Priority Review, which could shorten the review period to six months. If it does receive Priority Review, we could see Zepzelca approved for first-line maintenance in ES-SCLC patients before the end of 2025.

Partial legal win could lock Avadel’s Lumryz out of the idiopathic hypersomnia market

In late August, the United States District Court for the District of Delaware granted Jazz’s motion in part, preventing Avadel from seeking FDA approval or marketing of Lumryz for the treatment of idiopathic hypersomnia or “other indications not already part of Lumryz’s product labeling as of March 4, 2024, and enjoining Avadel from infringing in any way Claim 24 of the Company’s ‘782 patent by making, using or selling Lumryz through and including the February 2036 expiration date of the ‘782 patent.” The currently approved narcolepsy indication for Lumryz is excluded, and so are clinical trials ongoing as of the effective date of the injunction.

The Court also granted Jazz’s motion for an ongoing royalty on sales of Lumryz in the narcolepsy indication “at a rate to be determined based upon further briefing by the parties on the appropriate ongoing rate above 3.5%.”

I do not think either party is fully satisfied – Jazz sought to have Lumryz pulled from the market entirely, and Avadel sought to stay on the narcolepsy market and to expand to the idiopathic hypersomnia market.

As far as my investment thesis on Jazz is concerned, this is a clear win for Jazz because my baseline assumption was that the company would not be able to prevent Lumryz from being marketed for either narcolepsy or idiopathic hypersomnia. The latter market is particularly important since this is the indication Xyrem is not approved for and where Xywav should not be threatened by generic Xyrem (unlike the narcolepsy market where the authorized Xyrem generic is already having an impact on Jazz’s narcolepsy franchise). It is not easy to quantify the impact of this win, but keeping Lumryz out of the idiopathic hypersomnia market through 2036 likely preserves upwards of $1 billion in cumulative revenues for Jazz.

However, this may not be the final look of things for the two companies since Avadel is appealing this decision, and there is ongoing litigation between Jazz and the FDA because Jazz believes that the FDA violated the orphan drug exclusivity of Xywav by approving Lumryz for the treatment of narcolepsy.

Conclusion

Recent clinical and legal wins improve the long-term growth outlook for Jazz.

I believe Zepzelca’s phase 3 trial win in combination with Tecentriq represents an additional annual sales opportunity of at least $400-500 million a year for Jazz, and potentially more if the results (when they are presented) exceed my baseline assumptions.

On the legal side, the Court’s decisions on the dispute with Avadel put Jazz in a better position to defend Xywav’s market position in the idiopathic hypersomnia market and Jazz also gets to slightly benefit from Lumryz's narcolepsy sales in the form of a single-digit royalty rate. The caveat here is that the legal dispute is still far from over, and we could see additional changes after appeals and based on the outcomes of other ongoing trials.

The focus now turns back to quarterly sales updates where I do not expect significant deviations from the company’s full-year guidance or the Street consensus, and then, to the important phase 3 readout of zanidatamab in gastroesophageal adenocarcinoma (‘GEA’) patients in the second quarter of 2025. The results were pushed out by two quarters since this is an event-driven trial which needs to accrue enough disease progression events to complete. I have nothing new to add on this trial’s outcome to what I have already written – I expect Jazz to report positive results.

data.title: Verizon Q3 Earnings Preview: Frontier Won't Save The Day
data.description:
I am updating my analysis on Verizon Communications (NYSE:VZ) in advance of Q3 earnings, which will be released pre-market on October 22nd.

I previously rated Verizon a hold for the following reasons:

  • Cannibalization from the value segment risked core business growth
  • Costs could only be cut so far, and then margins would start deteriorating again
  • DCF generated price target suggested a small downside risk, although this was balanced against a strong dividend
  • Upside and downside risks were largely balanced

Since then, Verizon has returned 10%, while the S&P 500 has returned 7%.

I continue to be conflicted on Verizon, even more so in the light of the Frontier announcement. Earnings are likely to be underwhelming based on recent trends, and Verizon has a history of reducing shareholder value during major M&A deals.

On the flip side, Verizon continues its reign as the "premium" cell carrier and premium pricing along with it. In addition, there is the potential for upside if Verizon can successfully bundle and upsell customers between Fiber and Mobile.

While I lean slightly towards selling Verizon in the wake of the Frontier Deal, the compelling dividend and risk-reward profile must be considered. With that in mind, I maintain my hold rating on Verizon at a price target of $39.27.

Earnings Preview

Verizon is expected to announce EPS of $1.18 and revenue of $33.50 billion, which are each slightly up sequentially.

Analysts have been bearish on Q3 results, with 9 EPS down revisions and 0 up revisions over the last 3 months.

That said, Verizon has a history of guiding softer with 10 beats over the last 12 quarters. We can likely take consensus for what it's worth, following the down revisions.

Regardless, performance across the first half of the year, as well as Q3 consensus, keep Verizon solidly on track near the mid-range of 2024 guidance.

As quarterly results are issued, I will continue to focus on the core business and how the Value segment is impacting Verizon's ability to drive postpaid rate and volume. In addition, I am looking forward to hearing any more details on cost initiatives and the Frontier deal.

Valuation

I updated my ongoing DCF analysis on Verizon using the following assumptions:

  • Revenue growth of 2% based on management guidance, underlying trends in the business, and overall industry performance
  • Cost growth of 3.5% based on cost savings initiatives running out in the near term
  • The discount rate of 7% based on WACC of 6.5% adjusted up for deleveraging
  • Long-run growth rate of 1% to adjust industry expectations against Verizon's cost challenges

This DCF analysis yields a price target of $39.27, a slight downside from today's pricing, although still generating a generous dividend.

Wall Street is more bullish with a price target of $47.44, and my target is slightly outside the range, which leads me to exercise more caution when considering a final rating.

The quant rating is a solid hold, as low growth and poor revisions are offset by strong profitability and solid valuation multiples.

Frontier Deal Unlikely To Benefit Shareholders

On September 5th, Verizon announced they would acquire Frontier Communications in a deal valued at roughly $20 billion dollars.

Management stated the goal was to bring in a complementary fiber network, and it's true the networks are complementary.

However, I was immediately concerned by the deal.

Most important in my mind is that Verizon doesn't have a great track record with major acquisitions. Both Yahoo and TracFone deteriorated shareholder value from announcement to close date. Not to mention Verizon's purchase of Blue Jeans, which they shut down just a few years later.

The deal also seemed very expensive. Frontier was trading at $28.04 before the deal was announced, with $11 billion of debt (on $6 billion of revenue). The offer price of $38.50 per share represents a premium of 44% over the 90-day average value.

While management committed to $500 million of synergy value, it hardly justifies the price.

It is interesting to note that Frontier is still trading below offer value, indicating investor pessimism of the deal going through. And they are right to be pessimistic because the deal is now in jeopardy. Major shareholders, including the second-largest shareholder, are expected to vote no as they feel the deal is actually undervalued.

In my opinion, this is another expensive and unnecessary distraction for Verizon that will not add shareholder value, and may actually decrease it if history is any indication.

Upside Potential And Downside Risk

On the upside, Verizon still maintains a sizeable lead over competitors in pricing power and premium perception. In addition, their business unit has announced several large contracts with enterprise/government customers and more on the way. If management continues to execute in this space and drive the core business forward, there could be notable upside as my price target is highly sensitive to the revenue growth rate as I currently assume costs outpace revenue in the near term.

On the downside, the Frontier deal could play out like Yahoo, Blue Jeans, and TracFone, distracting from the core business and not driving value. This comes when management has already pulled every cost and margin lever available, so there may not be more to squeeze out. These factors could come together and depress margins, reducing shareholder value.

All in all, the upside potential and downside risk are balanced, especially in light of the Frontier deal being challenged by shareholders.

Verdict

I continue to struggle with Verizon's value proposition as they pursue one distraction for another, and seem to come out the other side at the same or lower value. Earnings are likely to be just fine now that consensus has been lowered. However, the Frontier deal is a wild card that requires caution.

At a DCF-generated price target of $39.27, slight downside from today, with balanced risk-reward and a healthy dividend, I cautiously maintain my hold rating on Verizon. Further underperformance in the core business as earnings are released could undercut this rating, and I will be monitoring closely next week.

data.title: RSPN: Industrials Dashboard For October
data.description:
This monthly article series reports aggregate subsector metrics in industrials. It is also a review of sector ETFs like Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI) and Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials ETF (NYSEARCA:RSPN), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for transportation in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in transportation companies. The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of last week's closing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by subsector (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

The value score has improved in transportation and deteriorated in other subsectors.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum based on median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

Based on my S&P 500 dashboard, the sector as a whole is overvalued by about 33% relative to 11-year averages. Machinery/conglomerates and transportation are overvalued by about 25%, while other subsectors are overvalued by 40% to 44%. Overvaluation may be partly offset by a high quality score for building/construction/electric equipment. Aerospace/defense is the least attractive subsector, with both value and quality scores significantly below the historical baseline.

Focus on RSPN

Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials ETF was launched on 11/01/2006 and tracks the S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials Index. It has 80 holdings and a management fee of 0.40%, whereas the capital-weighted ETF XLI charges only 0.09%. It looks quite expensive for a quarterly rebalancing of the same stocks.

All weights are equal after every rebalancing, but they drift with price action. They are now between 1.12% and 1.49%, while the heaviest position in XLI is 4.91%. The next table lists the top 10 holdings, representing 14.2% of asset value. These are the constituents with the highest price momentum since the last rebalancing.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a transportation company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0379 (or price/earnings below 26.39) is in the better half of the subsector regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. data.title: Consumption In Emerging Markets: Potentially Powerful Tailwinds Are Converging
data.description:
The Emerging Market (EM) asset class is comprised of 24 countries with various languages, economies, and political systems. Given this diversity, there are myriad factors that impact the group’s economic growth rates and equity market performances. Historically, however, three factors have had an outsized influence on EM performance: interest rates, U.S. dollar strength, and China. Recently, we have seen encouraging developments on all three of these fronts. This combination, in addition to EM’s favorable demographics and expanding consumer class, could be a significant catalyst to drive economic growth and consumption in EMs, making this an intriguing time for investors to reassess their EM allocations.

Key Takeaways

  • Between interest rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve, potential U.S. dollar weaknesses, and monetary and fiscal stimulus in China, EM equities may be poised to benefit from the convergence of three powerful tailwinds.
  • Consumption names within EM could be particularly appealing, as lower interest rates reduce individual debt burdens and encourage people to borrow and spend.
  • We believe that EM’s favorable demographics and rapidly expanding consumer class support the consumption theme’s long-term structural story.

The Stars Are Aligning

We see three powerful tailwinds aligning for Emerging Market assets. First, after a significant hiking cycle, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates by ½ a percentage point at its September meeting.1 Looking at 6- and 12-month periods following the end of Fed cutting cycles since 2000, Emerging Market equities have outperformed U.S. equities.

Second, the combination of easier monetary policy, incrementally slower U.S. economic growth, and a U.S. fiscal deficit not seen since the FDR Administration has led the market to reassess the sustainability of U.S. dollar strength.2 Historically, EM equities have returned roughly 3.5% for every 1% decline of the U.S. dollar.3

Third, China recently unleashed a powerful wave of monetary and fiscal stimulus, aimed to stabilize the economy and support consumption. This represents a meaningful change in policy for Beijing, with economic growth re-emerging as a government priority. Though the stars seem to be aligning for the asset class, we believe that consumption-oriented sectors should benefit most.

Bottom Up

From a bottom-up perspective, Emerging Market consumption names can often fall into the “growth” category. This is due to low penetration rates, attractive demographics, and bright prospects for long-term growth. For analysts attempting to discount cash flows to assess a company’s value, this means that much of the intrinsic value comes from long-term cash flows, making these companies especially sensitive to discount rates. As interest rates come down, mathematically (holding everything else constant), this implies higher price targets and potential market re-ratings. We’re additionally optimistic on EM companies levered with floating rate debt that should see net interest expenses decline and earnings increase as interest rates fall.

Top Down

Looking at EM consumption from the top down, lower interest rates become a powerful driver as the reduction in individual debt burdens plus a lower cost of capital incentivizes consumers to borrow and spend again. When looking at financial companies, lower rates can also have a strong benefit to asset quality, as borrowers should have an easier time paying back their loans. This, coupled with rising real wages globally, represents a potentially powerful tailwind for consumption.4

Long-Term Structural Story

Most Americans know the story of the U.S. baby boom. After WWII, birthrates in the U.S. surged, leading to population growth and increased demand for housing, education, healthcare, products, and services. This created employment growth and, in time, a significant expansion of the U.S. middle class. This growing middle class changed spending dynamics, as “Baby Boomers” looked to purchase homes in the suburbs, buy second cars, and take vacations. This created a virtuous cycle of employment opportunities, more discretionary income, and more consumption. The U.S. benefitted from 76.4 million babies born from 1946 through 1964.5 Global X believes there will be various similar economic patterns across Emerging Markets, as roughly five billion people are expected to be in the consumer class by 2031.6

A Differentiated Approach to Emerging Markets

Within consumption, we include companies from sectors including Discretionary, Staples, Financials, Healthcare, Communication Services, Information Technology, and Industrials. Global X believes that focusing on consumption trends not only allows an investor to benefit from structural (and often more predictable) stories, but it also allows active managers to reduce exposure to commodities, which could reduce overall exposure to the volatility of commodity prices, the unknowns in trade rhetoric, and the variability in foreign exchange swings. Ultimately, we aim to reduce volatility and improve risk-adjusted returns within our actively managed EM funds.

Footnotes

1. CNBC. (2024, Sep 18). Fed Slashes Interest Rates By a Half Point, an Aggressive Start to its First Easing Campaign in Four Years.

2. Bloomberg LP. Data as of September 27, 2024.

3. Global X ETFs analysis of data from Bloomberg LP. EM Equities are represented by the MSCI EM Index and the U.S. Dollar by the DXY Index. 12/31/1999 through 08/21/2024.

4. Trading Economics. (2024, October 2). Wage Growth Forecasts 2024/25.

5. Population Reference Bureau. (2014, Apr 16). Just How Many Baby Boomers Are There?6. World Data Lab. (2023, Jul 26). How The World Consumer Class Will Grow From 4 Billion To 5 Billion People By 2031.

Information provided by Global X Management Company LLC.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Diversification does not ensure a profit nor guarantee against a loss.

This material represents an assessment of the market environment at a specific point in time and is not intended to be a forecast of future events, or a guarantee of future results. This information is not intended to be individual or personalized investment or tax advice and should not be used for trading purposes. Please consult a financial advisor or tax professional for more information regarding your investment and/or tax situation.

Carefully consider the funds' investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses before investing. This and other information can be found in the funds' full or summary prospectuses, which may be obtained by calling 1-888-GX-FUND-1 (1.888.493.8631), or by visiting Home Read the prospectus carefully before investing.

data.title: Synchrony Financial 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Synchrony Financial in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: T-Mobile: Back In Time First, And Then A Look Forward
data.description:
By Levi at Elliott Wave Trader; Produced with Avi Gilburt

What Truly Moves Markets?

We follow the fundamentals with Lyn Alden. Fundamentals are important. However, they typically will frame a thesis for a 3 to 5 year time period. As well, we have observed that fundamentals rarely lead the stock price on a chart.

We’re going to go back in time, as it were, for a real-life case study in (NASDAQ:TMUS) as it unfolded over the past year. Along the way we had several updates as the stock progressed inside the anticipated structure. Then, we’ll take a look forward to project what is most likely for the chart. And, please note that this study is not based purely on the fundamentals of the stock. Why not?

Well, if it isn't fundamentals that lead, what is the driving force of important turning points in the markets? Sentiment. Simply put, fear and greed that battle against one another until the force of one overpowers the other.

Sellers will exhaust themselves at bottoms and buyers famously flame out at tops. All have observed fundamentals that get strenuously stretched to what appear to be absurd valuations to both extremes. Markets seem to be what we deem 'irrational' at these points. This is because markets are not logical, thinking entities. They are fluid, dynamic and non-linear in nature. Markets are sentiment in action before our very eyes.

Note this brief excerpt from an article that Avi Gilburt wrote regarding what truly moves markets. The piece was entitled, 'This Analysis Will Change The Way You Invest Forever'. In Part-2 (you can read it here) of the six-part series, Avi shared this point regarding Ralph Nelson Elliott's market research and findings:

In 1941, Elliott stated, regarding the financial markets, that "[t]hese [Fibonacci] ratios and series have been controlling and limited the extent and duration of price trends, irrespective of wars, politics, production indices, the supply of money, general purchasing power, and other generally accepted methods of determining stock values." As you can see, the more research conducted into this subject, the more support we find to Elliott's theories set out almost 100 years ago.

Every day, we take this theory and put it into practice for our members. This is illustrated across all markets and sectors. And, although the theory is just that, we believe that we have proven it’s dynamic utility and adaptability literally scores of times each week. Here’s just one way that we use it in a stock like TMUS.

Wave Setups Point To A High-Probability Scenario for TMUS

We have a regular feature that we simply refer to as "Wave Setups". These are charts that Zac Mannes and Garrett Patten will post along with specific targets and also invalidation points for each setup. Let’s track their progress starting back in August of 2023 when (TMUS) was featured as a bullish scenario with clear parameters for targets, support, resistance and invalidation of the setup.

From here, I would like to share just some of the key updates that were provided to members along the way. There were many more than what is in this portion of this piece, but I think you get the general idea of how the feature works.

Now, note in this latest update from a week ago that Zac is turning more cautious. Why is this? Bigger picture targets are being reached. What’s more, the highest probability portion of this bullish move is likely in place. Can the stock trend higher? Of course it can. But since we view the markets through a probabilistic lens, as Zac mentions, the risk vs reward skew is not favorable at this time.

So, What’s Likely Next For TMUS?

Well, I’m glad you asked. Let’s have a look at what the structure of price on the chart suggests as the probable path.

You can readily see from both of our lead analysts that we anticipate price to consolidate and pull back in a fourth wave. Since these can be highly variable in their structure, many may choose to stand aside as this issue works off the steam from the last bullish run. Third waves are typically the strongest of the waves in a 5 wave structure. So, yes, this can yet extend higher but we are turning cautious at this time.

It would seem that this anticipated consolidation could take several months and may lead to a correction to the $161 - $173 level. We will track the subwave structure of this decline once it begins to materialize in the larger degree. For now, it is expected to form an a-b-c, 3 wave structure for the fourth wave as you see on Zac and Garrett’s charts.

How Can We Use This Information?

This really depends on an individual’s time frame, risk tolerance, portfolio goals, etc. However, we can outline guidelines and then each person can protect, hedge, take profits, set stop losses and the like according to their own plan.

In this current case, should TMUS find a swing high here in the $218 area then the potential drawdown could be hedged with options or another similar strategy.

Conclusion

There are many ways to analyze and track stocks and the market they form. Some are more consistent than others. For us, this method has proved the most reliable and keeps us on the right side of the trade much more often than not. Nothing is perfect in this world, but for those looking to open their eyes to a new universe of trading and investing, why not consider studying this further? It may just be one of the most illuminating projects you undertake.

(Housekeeping Matters)

If you would like notifications as to when our new articles are published, please hit the button at the bottom of the page to "Follow" us.

data.title: Bank of America Q3: Why I Sold Shares After Earnings
data.description:
Yesterday, Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) released its fiscal third quarter earnings. The release beat expectations on revenue as well as on EPS. Going into the release, expectations were running high, as competitor JPMorgan Chase (JPM) had days prior put out earnings that handily beat expectations and ignited a rally in bank shares. It would have been very easy for Bank of America to disappoint following JPMorgan’s strong performance. Thankfully, the bank’s actual results pleased the markets and delivered a good price to sell at.

If you’ve followed my recent coverage of Bank of America stock, then you’ll know that I’ve been getting less bullish on it over time. It’s not that I think the company itself has deteriorated, or anything, but rather that the stock price has gone up too much. At this point, Bank of America is trading at 14.76 times earnings and 1.22 times book value. With $115 billion in unrealized losses, $296 billion in equity and 7.9 billion shares outstanding, the “adjusted” book value per share is 22.91, and the adjusted price/book is more like 1.83.

The above multiples are actually fairly high by bank standards. Although they are lower than those of the S&P 500 at the moment, banks usually trade at discounts because of heavy use of leverage and various regulations that limit how much of their earnings and assets can be distributed to shareholders.

BAC’s multiples are definitely higher than the norm for banking as a whole. For example, the 14.76 P/E ratio is higher than the 8.4-13.5 times earnings range that banks normally trade at. So, there is a case to be made that BAC is overvalued on a sector-relative basis. Seeking Alpha Quant seems to agree, giving BAC a merely-average ‘C’ on valuation.

Apart from the valuation concerns, there are concerns with Bank of America’s just-released earnings, too. Although the release was technically a beat and did trigger a minor rally in BAC shares, there were reasons to find the ‘absolute’ results underwhelming. The results showed a 10% decline in earnings, a tiny 1.3% jump in revenue, and very tepid growth in most segments. The increase in investment banking fees and the 1.8% q/q decline in share count were commendable, but the top and bottom line results were underwhelming.

When I last covered Bank of America, I rated it a hold because it was up dramatically after an earnings release I didn’t see as all that great. Now we have the stock again up after a just ‘so-so’ earnings release and trading above the historical norm for bank stocks as a whole. Seeing all this, I trimmed my position by 33% after the earnings release came out. In this article I will share the reasons why I trimmed my Bank of America position after earnings.

Earnings Recap

To kick things off, I'll share a summary of Bank of America’s third quarter earnings release, particularly the parts of it that led to my decision to sell BAC shares.

For the third quarter, Bank of America reported:

  • Net revenue of $25.3 billion, up 1.3% (a beat).

  • $0.81 in diluted EPS, down 10% (also a beat).

  • $14 billion in net interest income (NII), down 3% sequentially and up 2% y/y.

  • $1.5 billion in provisions for credit losses (PCLs), basically unchanged.

  • $114.7 billion in unrealized losses on debt securities.

  • $1.4 billion in investment banking fees, up 18%.

There were definite positives in this picture, but there were concerns as well. The unrealized loss remained among the largest of BAC’s peers who reported for the period. The high growth in investment banking looked nice, but the growth wasn’t anywhere near what JPMorgan did in the same period (31%). Finally, the persistently large unrealized loss was not a positive. Although I never thought that the large unrealized loss was an existential threat to BAC, it does make about a third of the company’s reported book value a mirage. So I consider the persistently large unrealized loss on BAC’s balance sheet a bad thing, one that must be compensated for.

Future Prospects

Having looked at Bank of America’s most recent quarterly results, we can now turn to the company’s future prospects. A good starting point here is the company’s guidance from its most recent earnings call.

In their most recent earnings call, BAC’s management team offered the following forward-looking statements:

  • Continued NII growth in Q4 (no specific figure).

  • A positive earnings impact from the BSBY acquisition.

  • Continued deposit growth.

The bank did not give specific numerical guidance for the fourth quarter but its management team did sound vaguely enthusiastic and upbeat on the call. Overall, I would expect a solidly profitable but growth-less Q4 based on what Bank of America’s management said on the call.

We can also turn to macro trends to help gauge what will happen with BAC in Q4.

We know that:

  1. The Fed is cutting interest rates, which should cut into the NII margin somewhat.

  2. Dealmaking at investment banks is up, and this can be expected to continue as lower rates make investors more eager to invest. So we’d expect the high growth in IB fees to continue.

  3. The wealth management business should also see increased client activity due to the aforementioned factors.

Given the mixed signals above, I’d expect Bank of America to put out earnings next quarter that are unchanged year-over-year. This is pretty close to what management hinted at on the call, so I feel the signals I’m operating off here are material.

Valuation

Now we can turn to valuing Bank of America shares in light of the recent earnings release and call.

First, here are some select multiples courtesy of Seeking Alpha Quant:

Some of these multiples appear appealing, especially when you compare them to the S&P 500. However, remember that banks are usually cheaper than the markets as a whole due to leverage and liquidity risks and various restraining regulations. Also, BAC’s price/book multiple increases to 1.83 when you adjust it for the unrealized loss on held-to-maturity securities. With these considerations in mind, BAC stock is not as cheap as it looks.

Since I’ve estimated 0% earnings growth for BAC for the near term, I can discount its cash flows based on that assumption.

Bank of America’s normalized EPS is $2.29. The 10-year treasury yield is 4%. I sometimes use risk premia as high as 5% when valuing stocks, but since I have many factors from both the earnings call and the economy to support my “0% forward growth for BAC” thesis, I’ll just use 1% here. So we’re discounting $2.29 at 5%. That produces a $45.8 fair value estimate–some upside, but not a whole lot.

Given that I acquired most of my Bank of America shares below $30, and that the stock is much closer to being fully valued today than it was when I bought it, I made the decision to trim my BAC at $42. At $45, I would exit the position.

The Bottom Line

Bank of America is doing reasonably well this year, but is close to being fully valued. By my estimates it’s worth somewhere between $45 and $46. I consider the stock a hold now and I’d consider it a sell at $45-$46. So, I’m happy to have trimmed my position.
